abstract: This article provides a critical examination of the concept of illiberalism, aiming to refine its definition for better application in empirical research. Moving beyond existing frameworks that often link the term exclusively to modern democratic backsliding or specific right-wing ideologies, the author proposes a structural definition centered on the rejection of three core liberal democratic pillars: limited executive power, state neutrality (impartiality), and the open society (universalism and individual autonomy). By decoupling the concept from specific institutional regimes or narrow temporal scopes, the study identifies illiberalism as a diverse ideational syndrome rather than a singular ideology. The second half of the paper delineates the conceptual boundaries between illiberalism, populism, and authoritarianism, and introduces a comprehensive typology comprising nine distinct "routes" to illiberalism: populist, authoritarian, traditionalist, religious, paternalist, libertarian, nativist-nationalist, materialist-technocratic, and left-wing. This multidimensional approach offers a more flexible and globally applicable toolkit for analyzing the various ways in which liberal democratic norms are currently being challenged.
